questions.stem: An RN delegates vital signs assessment to a UAP for four patients. Which patient's vital signs should the RN personally reassess rather than relying solely on the UAP's report?

options.text: A postoperative day 2 cholecystectomy patient with stable vitals
A patient with hypertension whose BP has been consistently 140-150/88-92 throughout the shift
A patient who recently returned from cardiac catheterization and now has a UAP-reported BP of 80/50 and HR of 118
A patient admitted for cellulitis with temperature reported as 37.8°C (100°F)

questions.rationale: When a UAP reports an abnormal or unexpected finding, the RN must personally assess and verify. A patient returning from cardiac catheterization with BP 80/50 and HR 118 requires immediate RN assessment — this could indicate arterial bleeding at the access site, cardiac tamponade, contrast reaction, or other life-threatening complication. The RN cannot manage this situation based solely on a UAP report; personal assessment, clinical judgment, and immediate intervention may be required. The other patients have findings within expected parameters for their diagnoses. The RN remains accountable for all delegated tasks and must respond to concerning reports.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave bleeding or anticoagulation injury untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "A patient who recently returned from cardiac catheterization and now has a UAP-reported BP of 80/50" fits the safer response to immediate intervention may be required.
distractorRationales.b: "A patient with hypertension whose BP has been consistently 140-150/88-92 throughout the shift" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "An RN delegates vital signs assessment to a UAP for four patients" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so bleeding-risk intervention is needed before diagnostic reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of bleeding-risk intervention. The stem cue "An RN delegates vital signs assessment to a UAP for four patients" makes bleeding or anticoagulation injury the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: When UAP reports an unexpected or critical finding, the RN must personally assess — do not manage clinical deterioration based solely on UAP-reported data.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient delivers vaginally and 30 minutes later has an estimated blood loss of 750 mL with a soft, boggy uterus displaced to the right. BP is 94/58, HR 118. Which sequence of interventions is correct?

options.text: IV oxytocin infusion, bimanual uterine compression, empty the bladder, call the provider
Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement
Call the provider, obtain type and crossmatch, begin blood transfusion
Place in Trendelenburg, administer IV epinephrine, call for hysterectomy consent

questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) from uterine atony management: (1) Massage the fundus — stimulates uterine contraction. (2) Empty the bladder — a full bladder displaces the uterus and prevents contraction (uterus displaced to the right = full bladder). (3) IV oxytocin — first-line uterotonic. (4) Notify provider if no response. Bladder emptying is critical and often overlooked — the displaced, boggy uterus signals bladder distension. Epinephrine (D) and hysterectomy are not indicated at this stage.

distractorRationales.a: "IV oxytocin infusion, bimanual uterine compression, empty the bladder, call the provider" misses the bedside risk because postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) from uterine atony management: (1) Massage the fundus — stimulates uterine contraction points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.c: "Call the provider, obtain type and crossmatch, begin blood transfusion" misses the bedside risk because postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) from uterine atony management: (1) Massage the fundus — stimulates uterine contraction points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; "Place in Trendelenburg, administer IV epinephrine, call for hysterectomy consent" moves care toward waiting for a callback before bleeding-risk intervention.

questions.takeaway: Boggy uterus displaced to the right after delivery = full bladder; empty bladder first, then fundal massage + oxytocin.

questions.stem: A post-operative patient on day 2 following abdominal surgery complains of left calf pain, warmth, and swelling. Vital signs are stable. Which action is the NURSE'S PRIORITY?

options.text: Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses
Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result
Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound
Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring

questions.rationale: Unilateral calf pain, warmth, and swelling in a post-operative patient are classic signs of deep vein thrombosis (DVT). The nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can be ordered promptly. Homan's sign (B) is unreliable and has been removed from clinical guidelines. Warmth and elevation (A) delay diagnosis. Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "A post-operative patient on day 2 following abdominal surgery complains of left calf pain, warmth" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.b: Because the nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can suggests circulation or perfusion, "Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: "Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine observation.

questions.takeaway: Post-op calf pain + swelling = suspect DVT; notify provider and anticipate duplex ultrasound — Homan's sign is unreliable.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A client with depression says: 'I don't see the point in trying anymore. Nothing I do matters.' Which nurse response is most therapeutic?

options.text: 'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.'
'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.'
'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.'
'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.'

questions.rationale: Option B is an open-ended exploration that invites the client to elaborate, demonstrating active listening without judgment. It also screens for suicidal ideation — 'nothing I do matters' and 'no point in trying' may signal passive suicidal thinking. Option A uses false reassurance ('so much to live for') and assumes understanding without exploring further. Option C minimizes the client's experience ('everyone feels that way'). Option D is non-therapeutic confrontation that dismisses the client's feelings.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "therapeutic communication safety" makes clinical safety risk the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "therapeutic communication safety" makes clinical safety risk the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.'" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "therapeutic communication safety" raises concern for clinical safety risk, so bedside safety action is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Therapeutic communication: use open-ended statements to explore meaning, especially when statements may signal suicidal ideation.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with pneumonia who is receiving 4L/min via nasal cannula. The SpO2 is 91%. The nurse repositions the patient and administers the scheduled nebulizer treatment. Which finding would indicate the interventions were effective?

options.text: Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95%
The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television
Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer
The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min

questions.rationale: Effective respiratory interventions produce measurable objective improvements in gas exchange: SpO2 rising to ≥ 94-95% and respiratory rate normalizing toward < 20/min indicate improved ventilation and oxygenation. Decreased anxiety may reflect improved oxygenation but is subjective and non-specific. Increased breath sounds after a nebulizer can indicate mobilization of secretions (which is initially normal), not necessarily a therapeutic response. Unchanged SpO2 and RR indicate no meaningful improvement from the interventions.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Unchanged SpO2 and RR indicate no meaningful improvement from the interventions" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: Because evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: Because work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring) suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.
visualRationale.conclusion: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing vital signs in the pediatric unit. Which set of vital signs is within normal limits for a healthy 3-year-old toddler?
questions.rationale: Normal vital signs for a 3-year-old toddler: HR 80–130 bpm, RR 22–37 breaths/min, BP approximately 90–100/55–65 mmHg. HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56 are all within expected ranges. Choice A has a bradycardic HR and adult-level BP. Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress, and RR 40 is elevated. Choice D has significant bradycardia. Key developmental milestone: vital signs normalize toward adult ranges with age — neonates have the highest HR and RR, lowest BP.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56" fits the safer response to toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "HR 145, RR 40, BP 80/48" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.d: Because pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60 suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "HR 55, RR 18, BP 110/72" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

visualRationale.title: Pediatric Nursing
visualRationale.caption: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.
visualRationale.conclusion: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

questions.stem: A patient is scheduled for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy at 8:00 AM. Per current ASA NPO guidelines, what are the correct restrictions for solid food and clear liquids?

options.text: Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid
Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery
Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, clear juice, black coffee) allowed until 2 hours before
No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk

questions.rationale: Current ASA NPO guidelines (2017, updated 2023): Solid foods, fried/fatty meals: NPO ≥8 hours. Light meals (toast, clear liquids): NPO ≥6 hours. Non-clear liquids including milk and formula: NPO ≥6 hours. Clear liquids (water, apple juice without pulp, clear carbonated beverages, black coffee or tea without cream, sports drinks): may be consumed up to 2 hours before elective procedures. Chewing gum and hard candy: treat as clear liquid — stop 2 hours before. These evidence-based guidelines replaced the outdated 'NPO after midnight' rule. The old rule increased patient discomfort, insulin resistance, and perioperative complications without reducing aspiration risk for clear fluids. High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses unstable glucose physiology; "Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid" moves care toward routine care before glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization.
distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization. The stem cue "ASA NPO: solids ≥6-8 hrs before" makes unstable glucose physiology the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water" fits the safer response to the old rule increased patient discomfort, insulin resistance.

questions.takeaway: ASA NPO: solids ≥6-8 hrs before; clear liquids until 2 hrs before. 'NPO after midnight' is outdated and harmful. Clear liquids: water, clear juice (no pulp), black coffee/tea.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing to administer insulin glargine (Lantus) and insulin aspart (NovoLog) to a patient with type 1 diabetes before breakfast. Which action by the nurse requires IMMEDIATE correction?

options.text: Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe
Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration
Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal
Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh

questions.rationale: Insulin glargine (Lantus) is a long-acting basal insulin with a specific pH formulation. It should NEVER be mixed with any other insulin, including rapid-acting insulins. Mixing glargine with other insulins alters its pH, causing it to crystallize and unpredictably changes both insulins' absorption profiles. The result is an unreliable pharmacokinetic profile that can cause severe hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia. Each insulin must be drawn in a separate syringe and administered as separate injections. Drawing up separately is correct. Injecting aspart before meals (pre-prandial) is appropriate. Site rotation is correct practice.

distractorRationales.a: "Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe" misses the bedside risk because a nurse is preparing to administer insulin glargine (Lantus) and insulin aspart (NovoLog) to a patient with type points to unstable glucose physiology. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization. The stem cue "It has its own pH and formulation" makes unstable glucose physiology the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "A nurse is preparing to administer insulin glargine (Lantus) and insulin aspart (NovoLog) to a patient with type" raises concern for unstable glucose physiology, so glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Glargine (Lantus) = NEVER MIX with other insulins. It has its own pH and formulation. Mix-up can cause crystal formation and unpredictable glycemic effect. Use separate syringes, separate injections. Applies also to detemir (Levemir).

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for four patients on a medical-surgical unit. Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL
A 44-year-old post-appendectomy (day 2) requesting pain medication, pain rated 5/10
A 71-year-old with heart failure who gained 1 lb overnight and is on daily weights
A 33-year-old with a UTI who asks when the next dose of antibiotics is due

questions.rationale: The patient with a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL has symptomatic hypoglycemia ('shaky'), which is an acute, life-threatening condition requiring immediate intervention. Untreated hypoglycemia can rapidly progress to seizures, loss of consciousness, and brain injury. The other patients represent important but lower-acuity concerns: the post-op patient has manageable pain, the heart failure patient's weight gain needs assessment but is not immediately life-threatening, and the UTI patient's antibiotic timing is a scheduled task.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of" fits the safer response to untreated hypoglycemia can rapidly progress to seizures, loss of consciousness.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization. The stem cue "Untreated hypoglycemia can rapidly progress to seizures, loss of consciousness" makes unstable glucose physiology the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses unstable glucose physiology; "A 33-year-old with a UTI who asks when the next dose of antibiotics is due" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization.

questions.takeaway: Apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine monitoring, and informational questions.

questions.stem: A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention. Which example correctly illustrates primary prevention?

options.text: Providing mammography screening to detect early breast cancer in women over 40
Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season
Prescribing physical therapy for a patient recovering from a hip fracture
Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps

questions.rationale: Primary prevention: actions taken before disease occurs to prevent onset. Examples: immunization, health education, smoking cessation counseling, fluoridated water, condom use. Secondary prevention: early detection of existing disease (screening) — mammography, colonoscopy, blood pressure screening, Pap smear. Tertiary prevention: reducing disability and restoring function in patients with established disease — rehabilitation, cardiac rehab, chronic disease management. Mammography and colonoscopy are secondary prevention (detecting disease before symptoms). Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention.

distractorRationales.a: "Providing mammography screening to detect early breast cancer in women over 40" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: "Prescribing physical therapy for a patient recovering from a hip fracture" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "Tertiary prevention: reducing disability and restoring function in patients with established disease — rehabilitation, cardiac rehab, chronic disease" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before diagnostic reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps" moves care toward routine care before circulation and perfusion support.

questions.takeaway: Primary prevention = before disease (vaccines, education); Secondary = early detection (screening); Tertiary = limit disability/restore function (rehab). Not to be confused with clinical levels of care.
